speaker: interviewer
text: October 11, 1995. Survivor is Israel Berkenwald and this is tape five. Israel, we left out something that we're going to go back. Would you talk to me about the appells in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first time that I have encountered appells, I didn't know what they mean, was in Auschwitz. In Auschwitz, the appells were one heck of a torture. It was around 4 o'clock in the morning-- it was still dark-- when we had to empty the barracks and stand at attention while we are being counted. At the same time, the [GERMAN], which means the man who was in charge of this particular barrack, had to assign a few people to take out the bodies. Because during the night, we always had 10, 12 dead bodies that died in the night, whether they committed suicide or whether they died because they couldn't take it anymore. But every night you had some dead people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then he had to assign a few people to take out the dead bodies. Dead bodies were lined up laying down on the ground, right next to where we were standing in appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: In each barrack in Birkenau-Auschwitz, we had approximately 700, 800 people in the barracks. So when we came out and we lined up, and we started the countdown-- we didn't count, the [GERMAN] counted-- sometimes we stood there for an hour, or two, or three, and especially when we were in Auschwitz, it was cold. It was cold, because we are at the foot of the Carpathian Mountains.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was cold. And we had only those uniforms that they gave us-- no underwear, no socks. It was cold. And in the morning, with dew and the mist.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time they were finished, we were half dead. And then he had to report how many live ones and how many dead ones, and the dead ones were immediately put on a hand carriage, and one or two people were carrying that hand carriage with about 10 bodies on it, that were picked up after appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody didn't stand straight at the appell? He got hit. If he made a face, he got hit. If he didn't count fast, he got hit. If he didn't have his head bent towards the next one to count, he got hit.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the appell was over, we really thanked God we came through another morning with the appell. In camp, we had the same appell. But in camp, they had to rush it up a little, because in camp, we were only sleeping, and they had to rush us to get into the factory. So still, an hour, an hour and a half appell, to count everybody, count the dead ones, count the live ones, and finally marching to the factory. You got a piece of bread and some mud called coffee, and you marched to the factory-- which was about 7, 8 kilometers away. You weren't taken by truck, or anything. And this repeated till-- itself, every morning. Every appell was terrible. But the worst appell was if one of the prisoners or slaves-- call it what you want-- tried to escape, and he was caught, or one of the prisoners escaped and the count at appell didn't match to the number of people that's supposed to be there. Then we stood and stood till really hell freezed over. When they finally caught him, they hung him in front of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Three times I saw it. Three times. Once there were two-- no, twice. Two, and once one. Caught and hung right in front of us, telling us, anybody that tries to do it, that's what's going to happen to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was really hard to escape unless you had some outside contact. You wore a uniform, a striped uniform, a striped hat, and wooden shoes. You know in Holland, they wear those shoes? Those are the shoes that we had. OK, thank you for going back with that. Let's go forward now. You were freed and you wanted to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was freed, and I wanted to go home, because in my mind, I couldn't possibly conceive-- it was beyond my comprehension-- that did I really, honestly lose everybody? Is it possible? Now, when I came to Lodz, I found-- I started looking-- I knew my older sister's dead. I knew my middle sister is dead. I knew that my father and mother is dead. I knew that my niece was dead. My nephew was dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: What about an aunt? What about an uncle? What about a cousin? I was with a cousin that died.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said, I must have somebody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't be just all alone in the whole world. So I figured the best thing for me would be, go back to Lodz, and start from the root up. I came back to Lodz--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by train. It took me about a week by train-- on top of the train. On top of the train I didn't have to pay. And as a matter of fact, I had a very close friend of mine, [NON-ENGLISH], that went with me. He was a little daredevil, and he stood up, and he was killed because we went under a tunnel. It actually was an overpass. He was hit and killed. And life was so cheap, I didn't even get off the train to try to help him, because the train was going fast. Finally, I arrived in a Lodz, and in Lodz, there was a Jewish agency called [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] had placards all over the walls with names. This one is looking for this one, and this one is looking for this one. And I was standing there and crying my eyes out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't I see somebody? Can't I see any known name? Anybody? Anybody? Didn't find anyone. So someone suggested, if you really think you want to find-- why don't you go into the state office, which is called a [GERMAN] and register yourself there. Maybe they registered themselves over there, they don't know that there's [GERMAN] here. So I went over there and I registered myself, and there were plenty of empty apartments, and I was able to go in there and sleep. And I gave my address where I was residing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about 10 or 12 days later, I get a card handed to me, by hand, I should report into the military barracks. I'm being drafted.

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, that was really too much. Add insult to injury? I just came out of camp? I'm being drafted already to the Polish army? And I definitely wouldn't serve in the Polish army, even if my life would depend upon it. So the next day, I was on my way back to Germany. When I came to Kielce-- also I started going back with the train. In Poland, I didn't pay. I really don't remember how I got onto the train. I went to the train station, I just took the train to Kielce, because I was told first to Kielce, then Krakow, and then from Krakow there's a border town called [NON-ENGLISH] which is very easy to cross, because the Russian command over there is looking away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in to Kielce. They're beating Jews, they're hitting Jews. You can't get off the train. I want to get off the train. I see people running towards me. And you know, it's very easy to recognize a Jew, especially right after the war. I didn't have yet the-- clothes, regular clothes. I still have-- the top was stripes, the pants were already civilians, but the top was stripes, because it was a jacket, like a shirt jacket. And I had underwear, so I put it over so it was warmer. I see them running, running towards the train. Don't get off! They're killing Jews! They're killing Jews! The Land Office Army. They called it the Land Army, [POLISH]. They're killing, killing Jews. Now again, I run back to the train, and now I'm riding towards Krakow. In Krakow-- we get off at Krakow, because from Krakow we had to walk already towards the border, or find any other way of transportation. In Krakow, I couldn't stay either. There was one building in Krakow, in the old part of town, that had a sort of haven for Jews that were on their way, leaving Krakow. You could stay there for a couple of days, and get a ration of food, you could get some money, as long as you registered yourself there. Which I did. I was in Krakow for two days. And from there, I was walking to the border. And it took about two days to cross the border into Czechoslovakia. In Czechoslovakia, I rode already. When I was in Czechoslovakia, I rode into Prague. In Prague, I registered myself again with the Jewish agency. I was given food and I was given clothes. I finally got rid of my striped jacket. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I was in Czechoslovakia, in Prague, for several days till I found someone who will know the way to go back into Germany, to the American zone because that was really the safest place-- was where the Americans were. Any other place, it was not safe. By the Russians it wasn't safe, by the Poles, it wasn't safe. You had to run away from every land you came into. Finally, I came into the American zone, and I went into a camp that was overloaded. And they told me at that time, near Frankfurt, a mine, a place called Zeilsheim, they are building a new camp. If I go there, I'll be one of the first ones, and I will get probably a room and a bed, and food. Which I did. Now, I-- I'm making it fast. It wasn't that easy to get from the border, through Munich, into Frankfurt, into Zeilsheim, but I made it. I mean, I got into Zeilsheim. There were only about 80 people and they were building a camp for that 150. Since I registered myself over there that I'm a bricklayer, they asked me to help out in building some more barracks. And eventually, the camp in Zeilsheim, DP camp, that was supervised by the UNRRA-- by the UNRRA-- and the Joint Distribution Committee, and supervised by Jewish Holocaust survivors. We had already several barracks. We built already a monument to our fallen families. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was an announcement that, anyone who wants to work for the American army should register. I registered immediately. I wanted to work, make some money, then maybe get the heck out of Germany as fast as I can.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I registered that I want to work for the American army. And they told me to 6 six o'clock sharp in the square, where I will be picked up the next morning. Sure enough, a truck came from the army and took me into Eschborn, which is about 12 miles from Zeilsheim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eschborn is an airport in Germany, near Frankfurt. It's a military airport. And I got a job on the airport. And I befriended some , Americans and one Jewish fella, Harry Schneiderman-- I became very, very friendly with, very friendly with. The reason why I became very friendly with him, he spoke fluent Yiddish. So I learned from an English-- from him English, because he spoke Jewish but with English words. Now, the word listen, until I realized what listen is, I knew already listen is listen. And I learned a little bit of English, which helped me, and I got a better job in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, I met about four months later. And we took to each other. I liked her very much. And then the captain of the base asked me if I would like to go to America. Sure, I would. He says, you know, for you it be-- it would be easier to go to America than for anyone else, because you already checked out by the CIA. Because anyone who wanted to go to America had to be checked out. Since I got a-- a job on the base, I didn't have to go through the CIA already-- I registered. And my wife and I decided at that time to get married. We were supposed to get married in April, and I met a friend of the family-- a very close friend of the family-- she-- I think-- I hope-- that she still lives in Israel. But she sort of talked me out of it. Well, I sort of gave her food, gave her money, gave her clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: She talked you out of what? Getting married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Getting married. You're too young. You don't know her long enough. You don't this, you don't that. I broke off with my wife. We were totally set to get married in April, and in May I was supposed to report to the boat already. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I left her, and I went to America. Once I came here, about three or four weeks later, I wrote her a letter. Come here-- don't go to England. Because she had papers to go to England, to her uncle. And I actually-- the first letter, I think, I pleaded with her. Please don't go to England, come here. I do love you. I want to be with you. I want to spend my life with you and-- best thing I ever did. She did come, and we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do in America? Just tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my first job was like a floor boy in a garment factory. Menial job-- I had no trade, really. But I didn't see any future in it. I saw more of a future in it if I should-- would learn how to sew. Then I have a trade. It was a dress shop, so the boss didn't want to give me a break to work at the machine, because I was very good on the floor. He promised to give me a raise and he gave me a raise. Stay on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't. So I left him. And somebody promised me to take me in to work-- do you know what section work is? Yeah, sort of.

speaker: interviewee
text: You make either a sleeve or a collar or a pocket.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to tell me your main work that you did a little later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I went to school. I finished school, then I was offered a scholarship to go to a division of Cornell University, which is later a college-- in the Empire State Building. And I took a test, and I qualified for it with flying colors-- colors. And when I finished, I was immediately offered a job with the ILGW, International Lady Garment Workers' union. I was offered a job as a price adjuster. You had to be very good with figures. Without bragging, I think I'm good with figures. And I was very good at it. And from there, I sort of went up the ladder. I achieved practically the highest point in the office without being the president of the union. And that is the job that I retired from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a little short message for your grandchildren, your great grandchildren-- which you haven't got yet? For the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- my message would be don't ever forget your background.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you for all that you've put into doing this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thank you. This is a picture of my aunt and uncle, from left to right is my cousin, Chaim, who was with me in camp and he died in my arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isn't that right? That's on the right. You said left to right. Do you mean the-- don't point. Do you mean the young man in the long trousers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The young man in a long trousers is my cousin, Chaim, who was with me in camp. And he died in camp. Next to him is his mother, Rifka. Next to him is my uncle, my youngest brother of my mother's, his name was Moshe. And next to my uncle is my cousin, Tora, who is alive and lives in Montreal, Canada. I got the picture from her. Sitting is my cousin, the youngest in that family. His name was Rick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your cousin in the long pants who died in the camp, what's his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His full name is Chaim Grossman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken in the summer place near Lodz, where we used to go during the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when do you think it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken about 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our wedding day. This is my wife, Jean, and next to her, it's I. That wedding happened November 2, 1947, in the Bronx, New York. This is a picture of my immediate family. It's my wife and I, my daughter, Elaine Klughaupt, Berkenwald, and my son, Martin Berkenwald. It was taken at the bar mitzvah of our middle grandson, Ezra Klughaupt in 1994, in South Orange, New Jersey. This picture is of my immediate family. From left to right, it is my son-in-law, Dr. Stanley Klughaupt, my oldest grandson, Zachary Zed Klughaupt, my middle grandson, Ezra Klughaupt, my youngest grandson, Jeremy Klughaupt, and my daughter, Elaine Berkenwald Klughaupt. The picture was taken in Vale, Colorado. They were out on a holiday, skiing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken 1994, in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce this lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my wife, Jean-- I'm privileged to be married to for the past 48 years. I hope we have a few more going.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give her a kiss.
